title: Mushroom and Thyme Toast

ingredients.name: mushrooms (button, cremini, or mixed), sliced or torn

subs.name: any wild or cultivated mushroom mix — shiitake, oyster and button together give excellent depth
subs.notes: tear rather than slice larger mushrooms for a better texture

subs.name: olive oil for a lighter, dairy-free version — use 2 tbsp
subs.notes: the flavour will be less rich but the mushrooms will still brown well

ingredients.name: fresh cream (for finishing)
visual_checks: adds richness; the dish is also lovely without it
ingredients.notes: adds richness; the dish is also lovely without it

subs.name: a spoonful of curd stirred in off the heat — adds a subtle tang rather than richness
subs.notes: do not boil the curd or it will split

subs.name: leftover chapatti or paratha, warmed and crisped on a dry tawa — the Indian adaptation works beautifully
subs.notes: pile on the mushrooms just before serving

ingredients.name: lemon
visual_checks: a small squeeze brightens the mushrooms
ingredients.notes: a small squeeze brightens the mushrooms

steps.text: Toast the bread slices until deeply golden on both sides. You want substantial, sturdy toast that won't go soggy when the mushrooms are piled on. Set aside on plates while you cook the mushrooms.

Melt the butter in a wide frying pan or tawa over high heat until it foams. Add the garlic and let it sizzle for 20 seconds, then add the mushrooms in a single layer. Do not stir for the first minute — let them sear and take on colour. They need high heat and space, not steam.

After 1–2 minutes, once the mushrooms have browned on the base, toss them and cook for 3–4 more minutes until golden all over and any liquid in the pan has evaporated. The pan should look nearly dry — wet mushrooms on toast are a disappointment. Season generously with salt and pepper.

Reduce the heat to medium-low. Strip the thyme leaves from the stalks and add them to the pan. If using cream, pour it in now and stir for 30 seconds until it coats the mushrooms in a light sauce. Squeeze a little lemon juice over everything and toss.

Pile the mushrooms generously onto the toasted bread. Scatter chopped parsley over the top if using. Serve immediately — the contrast between the crisp hot toast and the savour mushrooms should be enjoyed straight away.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: butter → olive oil for a lighter, dairy-free version — use 2 tbsp (the flavour will be less rich but the mushrooms will still brown well); cream → a spoonful of curd stirred in off the heat — adds a subtle tang rather than richness (do not boil the curd or it will split); mushroom → any wild or cultivated mushroom mix — shiitake, oyster and button together give excellent depth (tear rather than slice larger mushrooms for a better texture); bread → leftover chapatti or paratha, warmed and crisped on a dry tawa — the Indian adaptation works beautifully (pile on the mushrooms just before serving)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A non-stick tawa is ideal for getting the mushrooms golden without sticking. Amul butter browns beautifully and gives the right richness. Fresh cream (Amul) can be used in the finishing step. The dish is completely accessible with Indian pantry items. For an Indian twist, add a pinch of garam masala and replace parsley with coriander leaves — the result is a fusion mushroom toast that works brilliantly with pav.
